digest.top_line: PEACE-2: elective pelvic RT misses cPFS (HR 0.81, p=0.088), splitting from POP-RT (bPFS HR 0.50); whole-pelvis nodal benefit now contested.
digest.tldr: Prostate led with the elective pelvic-RT question splitting: PEACE-2 null on cPFS against POP-RT's positive biochemical signal. Breast brought long-horizon de-escalation reads (APBI, hypofx, boost/IM-MS omission). OLIGOMA delivered the first positive breast-oligomet MDT RCT (mPFS 35.8 vs 20.4, HR 0.48).

sites.intro: Elective pelvic nodal RT is the day's fault line: PEACE-2 null on cPFS while POP-RT stayed positive, and PACE-NODES/PIVOTALboost price the added nodal-RT bowel toxicity.

studies.name: POP-RT vs PEACE-2 Comparison
studies.tldr: WPRT cut biochemical failure in POP-RT (HR 0.50) but not PEACE-2 (HR 0.97); longer ADT and modern staging may blunt elective pelvic-RT benefit.
details: 🔍 Both phase III open-label RCTs: whole-pelvis + boost vs prostate-only IMRT in N0M0 high/very-high-risk prostate ca

details.text: 📊 WPRT vs prostate-only RT · HRs by trial (HR<1 favors WPRT)

details: 📐 Prostate RT dose: 74-76 Gy EQD2 (POP-RT) vs dose-escalated 78 Gy EQD2 (PEACE-2)
🔍 ADT duration: 2yr (POP-RT) vs 3yr (PEACE-2)
🔍 Staging: conventional CT/bone scan (POP-RT) vs PSMA PET/CT era (PEACE-2)
📊 Toxicity: comparable Grade ≥2 GU between arms in both; no increase with WPRT
⚠️ PEACE-2 is interim (median f/u ~5.5yr, ESTRO 2026); POP-RT updated at 6.3-7.2yr
⚠️ Cross-trial comparison, not head-to-head; era, staging, ADT, and dose all differ
🔗 Author's read: longer ADT + modern staging + higher dose may reduce elective pelvic RT's incremental benefit
studies.significance: The RT read is elective nodal coverage: PEACE-2's null WPRT signal (bPFS HR 0.97) carries three intensifications POP-RT (HR 0.50) lacked, 3yr vs 2yr ADT, 78 vs 74-76 Gy EQD2, and PSMA-PET vs conventional staging. Any of these could absorb the pelvic-RT benefit, so the divergence is confounded, not a clean refutation.
studies.monday_clinic: For a PSMA-staged very-high-risk N0M0 patient planned for 3-year ADT, this comparison supports questioning routine elective whole-pelvis RT; it does not extend to conventionally-staged, 2-year-ADT patients, where POP-RT's whole-pelvis benefit still holds.

Which very-high-risk subgroups still benefit from elective nodal irradiation?

studies.name: PRIME
studies.tldr: 5-fx SBRT (36.25Gy) shows no early inferiority vs 25-fx (68Gy) moderate hypofx, both + whole-pelvis nodal RT and 2y ADT, high-risk/N+ prostate.
details: 📊 1° EP biochemical FFS (Phoenix nadir+2): no early signal of inferiority for 5-fx SBRT at interim; mature 4-5y data pending
🔍 Phase III open-label non-inferiority RCT, N≈434, Tata Memorial + Indian centers; accrual 2018-2023; stratified by risk group + nodal status

details.text: 🔍 RT regimen by arm (both arms + whole-pelvis nodal RT + ~2y ADT)

details: 🔍 Both arms: whole-pelvis elective nodal RT + long-course ADT (~2y); modern IMRT/VMAT with daily IGRT
🔍 SIB to positive nodes allowed in the SBRT arm
📊 Grade 3+ GU/GI <1% in both arms (acute and late); no significant difference in grade ≥2 toxicity
🔗 vs HYPO-RT-PC (42.7Gy/7fx, 10y non-inferior): node-negative, prostate-only, no ADT, mostly 3D-CRT. PRIME extends 5-fx to N+/high-risk with whole-pelvis RT + ADT
⚠️ Interim only, 1-2y f/u; 'no signal of inferiority' is not established non-inferiority. Primary BFFS reads out at 4-5y
⚠️ BFFS is a biochemical surrogate; OS and MFS immature, no significant early difference

verdict.rationale: Interim analysis at 1-2y; primary BFFS immature, mature 4-5y pending. 'No early signal of inferiority' is not established non-inferiority; conclusion not yet drawable.
verdict.audience: High-risk, very-high-risk, or node-positive non-metastatic prostate cancer

studies.significance: The new ground is nodal RT in 5 fractions: PRIME gives whole-pelvis elective coverage at 25Gy/5fx (SIB to positive nodes) with 36.25Gy/5fx to prostate, pushing extreme hypofx into node-positive high-risk disease HYPO-RT-PC (node-negative, prostate-only, no ADT) never tested. Interim Grade 3+ GU/GI <1% both arms; BFFS non-inferiority waits on 4-5y.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk or node-positive non-metastatic prostate cancer already planned for whole-pelvis RT and long-course ADT, this interim read informs weighing 5-fraction SBRT as a shorter option; it does not yet extend to node-negative disease or to omitting ADT or nodal coverage.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 open-label non-inferiority RCT** (Tata Memorial Centre + Indian centers), 1:1, **N≈434**, stratified by risk group and nodal status. Accrual 2018-2023; **interim data at 1-2y**, mature 4-5y pending.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **High-risk, very-high-risk, and/or node-positive non-metastatic** prostate cancer; ECOG 0-2; life expectancy ≥10y. PSMA PET/CT staging allowed.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Both arms whole-pelvis elective nodal RT. **Arm A: 36.25Gy/5fx (7.25Gy/fx) to prostate, every other day**; **Arm B: 68Gy/25fx (2.7Gy/fx)** over ~5 weeks. Pelvic nodes **25Gy/5fx** both arms; **SIB to positive nodes allowed in SBRT arm**. Modern IMRT/VMAT + daily IGRT.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: **Long-course ADT (~2 years)** in both arms.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: biochemical failure-free survival** (Phoenix, nadir+2 ng/mL). Secondary: MFS, OS, cFFS, acute/late toxicity, QoL (QLQ-C30/PR25, IPSS), cost-effectiveness.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Interim: **no early signal of BFFS inferiority** for 5-fx SBRT; MFS and OS with **no significant early difference**, both immature. Mature 4-5y efficacy awaited.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: **Grade 3+ GU/GI <1% in both arms**, acute and late; no significant between-arm difference in grade ≥2 toxicity. QoL urinary and bowel domains stable and comparable.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Extends HYPO-RT-PC (42.7Gy/7fx, 10y non-inferior, node-negative, prostate-only, no ADT, mostly 3D-CRT) into **node-positive/high-risk disease with whole-pelvis RT + ADT** on modern IMRT/VMAT, a nodal-RT-in-5-fractions question that trial never addressed.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk, very-high-risk, or node-positive non-metastatic prostate cancer treated with whole-pelvis RT and long-course ADT. Does not represent node-negative, low/intermediate-risk, or ADT-omission populations.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Interim analysis at 1-2y follow-up; **primary BFFS immature**, so non-inferiority is not yet established. Open-label; late toxicity and durability read out at 4-5y.

open_questions: Mature 4-5 year BFFS non-inferiority of 5-fx SBRT
Late GU/GI toxicity of whole-pelvis nodal SBRT at 5+ years
Durability in node-positive vs node-negative high-risk subsets

studies.name: PACE-NODES
studies.tldr: Grade ≥2 GI toxicity 28% vs 21% adding pelvic nodal to prostate SBRT (5f); GU unchanged, primary efficacy endpoint immature.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, 1166 randomised, high-risk localised prostate; primary efficacy endpoint (biochemical/clinical failure) not yet mature
🔍 Both arms 36.25Gy/5f to prostate on alternate days; PPN adds 25Gy/5f elective pelvic nodal
📊 CTCAE grade ≥2 GI 28% (PPN-SBRT) vs 21% (P-SBRT) over 12 weeks
📊 No difference in acute GU toxicity, clinician- or patient-reported
📊 GI difference mirrored in EPIC-26 bowel domain at 4 weeks, resolved by 12wk
⚠️ 11% PPN-SBRT vs 4% P-SBRT did not receive allocation, mostly unmet dose constraints
⚠️ Acute-toxicity readout only (≤12wk); late effects and cancer control pending
🔗 Extends 5f prostate SBRT (established in low/intermediate-risk) to high-risk with elective nodal coverage

figures.caption: CTCAE grade ≥2 GI 28% (PPN-SBRT) vs 21% (P-SBRT) over 12 weeks.
P-SBRT 36.25Gy in 5f to prostate; PPN-SBRT adds 25Gy in 5f to nodes.

verdict.rationale: Primary efficacy endpoint (time to biochemical/clinical failure) not yet mature; reports only acute ≤12wk toxicity of adding pelvic nodal SBRT. Feasibility/safety readout, not the efficacy verdict.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised prostate (T3a-T4, Gleason 8-10, or PSA>20), planned ADT

studies.significance: The RT read is deliverability, not just the 28% vs 21% grade ≥2 GI: 11% of PPN-SBRT pts missed allocation on unmet planning constraints vs 4% prostate-only, a workflow signal for 5-fraction whole-pelvis. GI was transient (no difference by 12wk) and GU unaffected, so the tolerability cost of elective nodal coverage reads modest and short-lived.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk localised prostate choosing between 5-fraction prostate-only and prostate-plus-pelvic-nodal SBRT, this supports that elective nodal coverage is tolerable short-term (transient grade ≥2 GI, no GU cost); it does not yet establish whether nodal RT improves cancer control.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 RCT**, 1:1, **1166 randomised** (target n=1128), multicentre. The registered primary (time to biochemical or clinical failure) is **not yet mature**; this is the prespecified acute-toxicity analysis.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **High-risk localised prostate**: T3a-T4 and/or Gleason 8-10 and/or PSA>20 ng/ml, planned for **12-36 months ADT**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Both arms **36.25Gy/5f to prostate** on alternate days. **PPN-SBRT adds 25Gy/5f elective pelvic nodal**; P-SBRT treats prostate only. The nodal volume is the only intervention that differs.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary (this analysis):** CTCAE grade ≥2 GI and GU toxicity to 12wk. Patient-reported EPIC-26 domains at 4 weeks.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Grade ≥2 GI **28% (PPN) vs 21% (P)** over 12wk; **no GU difference** clinician- or patient-reported. GI signal mirrored in EPIC-26 bowel at 4wk and resolved by 12wk (no difference).

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk localised prostate cancer treated with 5-fraction SBRT plus 12-36 months ADT. Does not represent low/intermediate-risk, node-positive or metastatic disease, or conventionally fractionated nodal RT.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Acute toxicity only** (≤12wk); late GU/GI, failure and cancer-control endpoints pending. **11% of PPN-SBRT vs 4% of P-SBRT did not receive allocation**, mostly unmet planning constraints.

open_questions: Late GI/GU toxicity of elective pelvic nodal SBRT
Does adding pelvic nodal SBRT improve biochemical or clinical failure?

studies.name: PIVOTALboost
studies.tldr: Late 2yr G2+ bowel/bladder no higher with added pelvic-node RT + focal boost (20fx) than prostate-only; nodal RT's early bowel toxicity resolves by 18wk.
details: 📊 Late 2yr G2+ bowel and bladder similar across all three arms; adding pelvic nodes + focal boost did not increase durable toxicity (see table)
📊 Nodal RT raised early (<18wk) bowel toxicity vs prostate-only; the difference resolved by 18wk
🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1465 randomised at 39 UK centres; 1° endpoint biochemical/clinical failure (not reported in this analysis)

details.text: 🔍 Three randomised arms, all moderately hypofractionated 20-fraction IMRT
subdetails: Prostate + pelvic node IMRT + focal boost (PPN+B): 462 received

details: 🔍 Focal boost dose and boost/nodal dose levels not reported in source
⚠️ Secondary toxicity readout only; the primary efficacy endpoint (biochemical/clinical failure) is not yet reported
⚠️ 'Preliminary late' data: only 973/1314 (74%) had ≥2yr follow-up
🔗 vs FLAME (phase 3): focal boost improved biochemical control without added late toxicity; PIVOTALboost extends the boost to a 20fx schedule and adds elective nodal RT

verdict.rationale: Preliminary 2yr secondary toxicity endpoint; primary biochemical/clinical-failure readout not yet reported. Safety-only, so reassures on tolerability but establishes no efficacy.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised prostate cancer, moderately hypofractionated RT

studies.significance: Late tolerability, not efficacy, is the read: pelvic-node RT + boost at 20fx left 2yr G2+ bowel 6.5%, bladder 16.5%, no worse than prostate-only (8.2%/19.5%), with the early bowel bump resolving by 18wk. Weakens the late-toxicity case against elective nodal coverage in a hypofractionated schedule.
studies.monday_clinic: In a high-risk localised prostate patient on a 20-fraction course, the tolerability case for elective pelvic nodal RT plus a focal boost holds through 2 years, so late GU/GI toxicity is not a reason to default to prostate-only.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 RCT**, N=1465 randomised at 39 UK centres, moderately hypofractionated **20-fraction** schedule. Primary endpoint is biochemical/clinical failure; this presentation reports only the **secondary** acute (<18wk) and preliminary late (2-year) bowel and bladder toxicity endpoints.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Localised prostate cancer; the source flags the **high-risk localised** group as most likely to benefit from a focal boost. Full eligibility and baseline characteristics not reported in the presentation.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Three arms delivered in **20 fractions**: prostate IMRT alone, prostate IMRT + focal boost, and prostate + **pelvic node** IMRT + focal boost. Focal boost dose and boost/nodal dose levels **not reported** in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** biochemical/clinical failure (not reported here). **Secondary (reported):** early (<18 weeks) and late (2-year) G2+/G3+ bowel and bladder toxicity.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Pelvic node RT raised early (<18wk) bowel toxicity that **resolved by 18 weeks**. Late 2-year G2+ bowel and bladder rates were **similar across all three arms** (see figure); adding pelvic nodes + boost did not increase durable toxicity.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Builds on **FLAME** (phase 3), where a focal boost improved biochemical control without added late toxicity in conventional fractionation. PIVOTALboost tests the same boost, plus elective nodal RT, in a 20-fraction backbone.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk localised prostate cancer treated with moderately hypofractionated (20fx) IMRT. Does not represent conventionally fractionated schedules or metastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Preliminary late** data: only **973/1314 (74%)** had ≥2 years' follow-up. Reports secondary toxicity only, so efficacy of the boost and nodal RT is **unestablished** pending the primary biochemical/clinical failure readout.

open_questions: Efficacy of focal boost plus pelvic node RT on biochemical/clinical failure
Elective pelvic nodal RT benefit in high-risk localised prostate cancer

studies.name: PEACE-2
studies.tldr: Pelvic RT gave no significant cPFS benefit vs prostate-only RT: HR 0.81 (0.63-1.03), p=0.088; 7yr cPFS 67.1% vs 62.9%.
details: 🔍 2×2 factorial RCT (GETUG/Unicancer, international multicenter): pelvic vs prostate-only RT, crossed with ± cabazitaxel ×4; ADT ×3yr backbone
🔍 Very high-risk localized N0M0: ≥2 of Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20 ng/mL; staged by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT
📊 Pelvic nodal RT did not significantly improve cPFS vs prostate-only; 1° endpoint not met (p=0.088)
🔗 Diverges from POP-RT (JCO 2021), where whole-pelvic RT improved biochemical failure-free survival in high-risk N0 pts
🔗 vs RTOG 9413: whole-pelvic RT benefit long inconsistent; PEACE-2 adds a modern null in the PET-eligible staging era
⚠️ cPFS is a surrogate, not OS; CI 0.63-1.03 can't exclude a modest pelvic-RT benefit, and the numeric trend favored pelvis
⚠️ <1 in 10 men died of prostate cancer by 10yr, questioning the 'very high-risk' label, esp if PSMA PET N0
🔍 Cabazitaxel arms (C/D) results not shown in this presentation

verdict.rationale: Randomised, prespecified cPFS primary, large N; null pelvic-RT result diverges from POP-RT's positive whole-pelvic signal. Design internally valid, so the divergence counts.
verdict.audience: Very high-risk localized N0M0 prostate: Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, or PSA ≥20

studies.significance: The elective-nodal decision: whole-pelvic RT gave a non-significant 7yr cPFS edge (67.1% vs 62.9% prostate-only, HR 0.81, p=0.088), opposite POP-RT's positive read. In PET-staged very-high-risk N0M0 pts, routine pelvic nodal irradiation is not clearly earning its toxicity. Dose/fractionation not in source.
studies.monday_clinic: In very-high-risk N0M0 localized prostate cancer (Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, or PSA ≥20, PET/CT node-negative), this questions routine elective pelvic nodal RT over prostate-only; it does not extend to node-positive or metastatic disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3** 2×2 factorial RCT (GETUG/Unicancer, international multicenter). Randomizes pelvic vs prostate-only RT, crossed with ± cabazitaxel ×4. **Primary endpoint: cPFS.** 380 (prostate-only) vs 381 (pelvic) at risk in the RT comparison.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Very high-risk **localized, N0M0** prostate cancer with **≥2 of**: Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20 ng/mL. Nodal status by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: The comparison is **target volume**: prostate-only vs whole-pelvic (elective nodal) RT. Dose, fractionation, and nodal CTV not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Primary: cPFS. Secondary: 3-mo PSA response, bPFS, metastasis-free survival, prostate-cancer-specific survival, OS, tolerance, QoL, biopsy biomarkers. OS not reported in this presentation.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Pelvic nodal RT **did not meet** the cPFS primary: HR 0.81 (95% CI 0.63-1.03), p=0.088. The 7yr absolute difference favored pelvic RT but was not significant.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **POP-RT** (JCO 2021) found whole-pelvic RT improved biochemical failure-free survival in high-risk N0 pts; PEACE-2 is **null on cPFS**, reopening the elective-nodal question.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents very-high-risk, node-negative (N0M0), non-metastatic localized prostate cancer staged by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT. Does not represent node-positive, M1, or exclusively PSMA-PET-staged populations, nor low/intermediate-risk disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: cPFS is a surrogate, not OS; the trial was **not powered** to exclude a small pelvic-RT benefit (CI to 1.03). RT dose, fractionation, and nodal volumes not in source.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: With **<1 in 10** men dying of prostate cancer by 10yr, the presenters question whether these pts are truly 'very high-risk', especially when PSMA PET/CT is N0, and argue for biomarker-guided intensification or de-intensification over blanket pelvic RT.

open_questions: Does pelvic nodal RT help PSMA-PET-staged N0 very-high-risk pts?
Biomarkers to guide intensification vs de-intensification
Do the cabazitaxel arms (C/D) improve outcomes?

sites.intro: Long-horizon de-escalation dominates: 15-20yr APBI, moderate hypofx, boost and IM-MS omission reads, plus OLIGOMA's first positive breast-oligomet MDT RCT.

studies.name: OLIGOMA
studies.tldr: mPFS 35.8 vs 20.4 mo, HR 0.48 (0.25-0.91), p=0.021 favoring add-on ablative RT to all lesions in oligometastatic breast.
details: 🔍 First RCT adding metastasis-directed RT (ablative to all lesions) to systemic therapy in oligometastatic breast cancer (≤5 mets, any line)
🔍 Co-primary EPs: PFS and QLQ-C30 QoL at 12wk; stratified by systemic-therapy type and treatment line
🩻 >80% had 1-3 mets, 2/3 of lesions bone; mostly ER/PR+ HER2- in the first-line setting
📊 QoL co-primary non-inferior at 12wk: ablative RT to all mets didn't degrade short-term QoL
⚠️ Stopped early: <20% of initial, <40% of amended accrual target (43 exp / 44 control). Small trial, wide CI (HR upper bound 0.91)
⚠️ PFS is a surrogate; OS not reported in source. Open-label (MDT vs no-MDT can't be blinded)
🔗 SABR-COMET (mixed histology) first signaled an MDT survival benefit; OLIGOMA is the first positive RCT specific to breast oligomets
⚠️ RT dose, fractionation, and modality of the 'local ablative RT' not specified in source
🔍 Confirmatory breast-MDT trials still accruing: TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA, CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI

figures.caption: 12-wk QLQ-C30: 72.2 (67.2-77.2) exp vs 74.3 (69.3-79.3) control; between-group Δ -2.1 (-9.2-5.1), NI margin -10.

verdict.rationale: First RCT of MDT in breast oligomets; randomized, co-primary PFS hit vs systemic alone. Aligns with SABR-COMET signal. Stopped early, small N, so not practice-changing.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic breast, ≤5 mets, mostly ER/PR+ HER2-, first-line systemic

studies.significance: The QoL co-primary is the quiet win: ablating ALL lesions (not just symptomatic) left 12-wk QLQ-C30 non-inferior (Δ -2.1, NI margin -10), so the PFS gain (HR 0.48) doesn't cost short-term QoL. Moves the decision from palliative-RT-for-symptoms toward comprehensive ablative MDT in ER/PR+ first-line breast oligomets. Dose/fractionation unreported, so technique transfer is unresolved.
studies.monday_clinic: In ER/PR+ HER2- breast cancer with 1-5 (mostly bone) mets on first-line systemic therapy, this supports comprehensive ablative MDT over RT reserved for symptomatic sites; it does not extend to polymetastatic disease or establish an OS benefit.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized RCT (ARO-2021-09), 1:1, systemic therapy alone vs systemic therapy **+ local ablative RT to all metastatic lesions**. Stratified by systemic-therapy type and treatment line. **Stopped early** at <20% of initial and <40% of amended accrual target.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Metastatic breast cancer, any treatment line, **maximum 5 lesions**. >80% had 1-3 mets, 2/3 of lesions bone. Majority **ER/PR+ HER2-**, first-line; median age 58-59. Pts requiring palliative RT to all mets were excluded.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Experimental arm delivered **local ablative RT to ALL metastatic lesions**; palliative RT to symptomatic mets allowed in both arms. **Dose, fractionation, and modality not reported in source.**

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Co-primary:** PFS and QoL (EORTC QLQ-C30) at 12wk. Secondary: overall survival, toxicity, compliance, QLQ-BR23, patient satisfaction (PATSAT C33).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Both co-primary endpoints favorable: PFS improved (**HR 0.48, p=0.021**) and 12-wk QoL **non-inferior**. Per-arm medians in the KM figure.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: First positive RCT of MDT specific to breast oligomets. **SABR-COMET** (mixed histology) previously signaled an MDT survival benefit; numerous breast-specific trials (TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA, CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI) are still accruing.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents oligometastatic (≤5 lesions) ER/PR+ HER2- breast cancer, bone-predominant, on first-line systemic therapy. Does not represent polymetastatic disease, HER2+ or triple-negative-dominant cohorts, or pts needing palliative RT to all sites.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Stopped early with small N (43 vs 44), so the CI is wide (**HR upper bound 0.91**) and PFS is a surrogate with OS not reported. Open-label; RT technique unspecified, limiting transfer.

open_questions: Which oligometastatic breast subgroups benefit most from MDT

studies.name: APBI-IMRT Florence
studies.tldr: 15yr IBTR 7.7% vs 4.2% (APBI 30Gy/5fx vs WBI+boost), HR 1.57 (0.82-3.04) p=0.17; excess is new primaries, not true relapse.
details: 📊 1° EP IBTR at 15yr: HR 1.57 (95% CI 0.82-3.04), p=0.17 — numerically higher with APBI, not significant
🔍 Phase III equivalence RCT, N=520, 1:1; enrolled 2005-2013; median f/u now 15yr (Livi EJC 2015, Meattini JCO 2020)
📐 APBI 30Gy/5fx IMRT to partial breast vs WBI 50Gy/25fx + 10Gy/5fx tumour-bed boost
📊 Authors: the IBTR excess is new ipsilateral primaries, not true local relapse
⚠️ New-primary vs true-relapse is a soft distinction at 15yr; the absolute IBTR gap is real to the pt regardless
⚠️ Powered for 5yr IBTR equivalence (Δ5%, 80% power); wide 15yr HR CI (0.82-3.04) can't exclude a real local-control decrement
🔗 No difference in locoregional control, distant mets, breast-cancer-specific or overall survival at 15yr

verdict.rationale: Phase 3 equivalence RCT with mature 15yr f/u backing an already guideline-listed de-escalation; aligns with the APBI evidence base rather than overturning practice.
verdict.audience: Selected early breast ca, pT<25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40, post-BCS

studies.significance: The RT read is local control: true in-field relapse stayed flat (2.1% vs 1.6% at 15yr), and the numerical IBTR excess is new ipsilateral primaries the partial-breast volume never covered. Supports 30Gy/5fx APBI as durable de-escalation in pT<25mm, margin-negative, age>40 breast, though powered only at 5yr (15yr CI 0.82-3.04 stays wide).
studies.monday_clinic: In selected early breast cancer (unifocal pT <25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40 after BCS), 15yr outcomes support 30Gy/5fx partial-breast IMRT as an alternative to whole-breast+boost; it does not extend to age ≤40, tumours >25mm, or close margins the trial excluded.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 equivalence RCT, 1:1, N=520, enrolled 2005-2013; median follow-up now 15yr. **Primary endpoint IBTR**, powered on a 5yr estimate ~3% with a **5% equivalence margin**, 80% power.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Selected early breast cancer after breast-conserving surgery: **pT <25mm, final surgical margins ≥5mm, age >40**. 260 per arm.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: APBI arm **30Gy in 5 fractions IMRT** to the partial breast; control **whole-breast 50Gy/25fx + 10Gy/5fx tumour-bed boost**.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Beyond IBTR (see table), no 15yr difference in locoregional recurrence, distant metastasis (7 vs 12 events), or deaths (56 vs 51); breast-cancer-specific and overall survival equivalent.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Consistent with the APBI evidence base (IMPORT LOW, NSABP B-39/RTOG 0413) supporting partial-breast irradiation as non-inferior for local control in selected pts; Florence uses one of the shortest EBRT-APBI schedules (5fx).

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents selected early breast cancer (pT <25mm, margins ≥5mm, age >40) after breast-conserving surgery. Does not represent age ≤40, tumours >25mm, or close margins excluded by the entry criteria.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Powered for **5yr equivalence**, so the 15yr HR CI (**0.82-3.04**) is wide and can't exclude a true local-control decrement. New-primary vs true-relapse split softening the IBTR signal is an interpretive call.

studies.name: DBCG RT Natural
studies.tldr: Invasive LR 1.5% with PBI vs 9.8% without at median 4y in low-risk elderly breast; omitting both RT+ET → 12.2%.
details: 🔍 Phase III randomized: PBI 40Gy/15fr vs no PBI, stratified by institution and ET use
🔍 Eligible: ≥60y, pT1N0, unifocal non-lobular, ER≥10%, HER2-normal, grade 1-2, margin ≥2mm, post-BCS
🔍 1° EP: 5-yr invasive LR (assumed 2%, max acceptable 4%); planned N=926
⚠️ First/interim results at median 4y FU (5-yr primary immature); stopped on planned rules, so magnitude may be inflated
📊 41 invasive LR total (36 isolated), all invasive; 39 of 41 occurred in no-PBI pts
📊 Only 4 distant failures (2 +RT, 2 no PBI); no survival signal reported in source
⚠️ Real-world ET adherence often suboptimal; PBI delivers local control independent of adherence
🔗 vs PRIME II / LUMINA (both support RT omission in low-risk elderly on ET): DBCG counters that omitting both RT+ET is unsafe
🔗 vs EUROPA (PBI vs endocrine in elderly low-risk): RT arm had better QoL (per curator note)

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase III but first/interim readout at median 4y (5-yr primary immature), stopped on planned rules; direction reliable, magnitude not yet final.
verdict.audience: Women ≥60, pT1N0 grade 1-2 ER+/HER2-normal breast, post-BCS

studies.significance: The RT read is the 2×2: PBI alone (3.0%) and ET alone (3.7%) give near-identical local control, both far below the omit-everything arm (12.2%). Because real-world ET adherence is often poor, PBI (40Gy/15fr) offers adherence-independent control. Moves the omit-PBI decision toward keeping RT when endocrine therapy can't be assured.
studies.monday_clinic: In women ≥60 with pT1N0, grade 1-2, ER+/HER2-normal breast after breast conservation, this questions omitting PBI when endocrine therapy is declined or adherence is uncertain; it does not extend to node-positive, grade 3, younger, or lobular pts.

primary_endpoint.name: 5-year invasive local recurrence
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: CIF 1.5% (0.3-5.1) vs 9.8% (5.9-14.9); median 4y FU, 5y EP immature

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 randomized (**PBI 40Gy/15fr vs no PBI**), stratified by institution and endocrine-therapy use; planned **N 926**. First results are an **interim/early-stopped** readout at **median 4y** FU, with the 5-year primary still immature.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **≥60y, pT1N0**, unifocal, non-lobular, **ER≥10%, HER2-normal, grade 1-2**, limited DCIS, **margin ≥2mm**, post-breast-conservation. Endocrine therapy per DBCG guideline (recommended for pT1c and/or grade 2).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Partial breast irradiation, 40Gy/15fr**, versus no PBI. A third **self-selecting no-PBI** cohort was followed observationally, not randomized.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: 5-year invasive local recurrence** (assumed 2%, max acceptable **4%**). Secondary: loco-regional side effects and QoL, not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: PBI reduced invasive local recurrence versus omission; RT alone and ET alone gave **similar** control while omitting both fared worst and **RT+ET had no recurrences**. Only **4 distant failures** overall, no survival signal.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **PRIME II / LUMINA** support omitting RT in low-risk elderly on endocrine therapy; DBCG RT Natural pushes back, showing PBI still lowers recurrence and that **omitting both RT and ET is unsafe**. **EUROPA** favored RT over endocrine therapy on QoL.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents women ≥60 with **pT1N0, grade 1-2, ER+/HER2-normal** breast cancer after breast conservation. Does not represent **node-positive, grade 3, younger, or lobular** patients.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Interim, median 4y** FU (5-year primary immature), so effect size may be inflated. The **no-PBI self-selecting arm is non-randomized**. QoL and toxicity secondaries not reported in source.

studies.name: IMPORT HIGH
studies.tldr: 10yr IBTR 3.7% (48Gy SIB) vs 3.5% (sequential 40+16Gy) vs 5.5% (53Gy); SIB non-inferior, further escalation not advantageous.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, 1:1:1, N=2617, 76 UK hospitals (2009-2015); higher-than-average local-relapse risk group

details.text: 🔍 Three RT arms, all delivered over 3 weeks
📊 OS absolute difference at 10yr vs 40Gy/15F (both CIs cross 0)
📊 Moderate/marked late effects at 10yr, similar across all arms

details: ⚠️ Further escalation to 53Gy not advantageous: numerically higher relapse, no OS or toxicity offset
🔗 Both 40+16Gy and 48Gy arms stayed below the 5% control estimate used in the original power calculation
🔍 SIB = single-phase 15-fraction course over 3 weeks, fewer patient visits than a sequential boost

verdict.rationale: Phase 3 RCT 10yr local-control update confirming 5yr non-inferiority of 48Gy SIB; reaffirms current practice, and escalation to 53Gy adds no benefit.
verdict.audience: Early invasive breast (pT1-3 pN0-3a M0), BCS + tumour-bed boost

studies.significance: The durability is the read: 48Gy/15F SIB stays non-inferior to the sequential 40Gy+16Gy boost at 10yr (IBTR 3.7% vs 3.5%), while escalating to 53Gy costs local control (5.5%) with no OS or late-toxicity offset. Backs 48Gy SIB in 15 fractions over 3 weeks as the boost standard, no further escalation.
studies.monday_clinic: In higher-risk early invasive breast cancer (pT1-3 pN0-3a) after BCS needing a tumour-bed boost, this supports a 48Gy/15F simultaneous integrated boost over a separate sequential boost and argues against escalating to 53Gy; it does not speak to boost omission or lower-risk pts who need no boost.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 RCT, 1:1:1, N=2617** across 76 UK hospitals (2009-2015). Ten-year update of the 5-year non-inferiority result (Coles, Lancet 2023). Annual clinical follow-up to 10 years; PROs and photographs to 5 years.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Women ≥18 after breast-conserving surgery for **invasive early breast cancer (pT1-3, pN0-pN3a, M0)** requiring a tumour-bed boost. Described as a **higher-than-average local-relapse risk** group.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Control: **40Gy/15F** whole breast + sequential **16Gy/8F** boost. SIB arms: **48Gy/15F** (3.2Gy/F) or **53Gy/15F** (3.5Gy/F), boosting highest-risk regions with a modest dose reduction to whole breast distant from tumour. All delivered in 3 weeks.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: ipsilateral breast tumour relapse (IBTR).** Secondary: overall survival, late normal-tissue effects, PROs.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **48Gy SIB non-inferior** to the sequential boost at 10yr; **escalation to 53Gy gave no advantage** (numerically higher relapse). OS: **no difference** between arms (CIs cross zero).

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Moderate/marked late effects at 10yr similar across arms: breast distortion/shrinkage <18%, induration <10%, telangiectasia <2%, breast oedema <2%.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Confirms the 5yr non-inferiority (Lancet 2023: 5yr IBTR 1.9% / 2.0% / 3.2%). Event rates in the 40+16Gy and 48Gy arms stay **below the 5% control estimate** used in the original power calculation.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents higher-risk early invasive breast cancer (pT1-3, pN0-3a) after BCS selected for a tumour-bed boost. Does not represent pts eligible for boost omission or those not requiring a boost.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Non-inferiority framing, not powered to test superiority of escalation. PRO and photographic assessment only to 5 years, not 10.

open_questions: Durability of 48Gy SIB beyond 10 years
SIB in ultra-hypofractionated (5-fraction) schedules
Which pts can safely omit the boost

studies.name: EORTC IM-MS trial (22922/10925)
studies.tldr: 20y OS neutral (61.0% vs 61.8%, HR 1.00): IM-MS RT cut breast-cancer mortality but excess non-BC deaths cancelled the survival gain.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT (EORTC 22922/10925): IM + medial supraclavicular nodal RT vs no IM-MS RT, added to standard breast/chest-wall RT.
🔍 Stage I-III breast, ~2002 pts/arm, 20-year ITT follow-up.
🔍 RT dose, fractionation, and target-volume detail not reported in source.
📊 pN0 subgroup (20y): no IM-MS RT benefit
🔗 Heart doses ran 4-9× higher than the contemporary DBCG IMN2, so the era's toxicity (not the concept) drove the excess non-BC deaths.
🔗 DBCG IMN2 (modern heart-sparing, MHD 1.2 Gy right / 2.3 Gy left): IM-MS RT cut distant mets + BCM and improved OS in node+ pts at 15y (Nielsen, Lancet Reg Health Eur 2024).
⚠️ No difference in second cancers or second breast cancers between arms.
⚠️ Net OS null (HR 1.00) reflects a real BCM benefit cancelled by excess cardiac/pulmonary mortality, not absence of anticancer effect.

verdict.rationale: Mature prespecified phase 3 RCT with null 20y OS primary, contesting the survival rationale for IM-MS nodal RT; divergence is design-valid, the toxicity counterweight technique-dependent.
verdict.audience: Stage I-III breast cancer, adjuvant setting (pN0 and node-positive)

studies.significance: IM-MS RT still cut 15y breast-cancer mortality (HR 0.82); the older technique's excess cardiac/lung deaths (non-BCM HR 1.26) erased net OS, not a failed anticancer effect. With DBCG IMN2 heart doses 4-9× lower, the benefit should hold, so this supports retaining internal-mammary coverage in node-positive disease under tight heart-dose constraints.
studies.monday_clinic: In node-positive breast cancer planned for regional nodal RT with heart-sparing technique, this 20y update supports internal-mammary coverage; it does not extend to pN0 disease, where added IM-MS RT left 20y DFS and DMFS unchanged (HR 0.93, ns).

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 RCT (EORTC 22922/10925), stage I-III breast cancer, ~**2002 pts/arm**. IM plus medial supraclavicular nodal RT added to standard breast/chest-wall RT vs none. **20-year** ITT follow-up.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Dose, fractionation, and target volume not reported in source.** Heart exposure was high for the era, running **4-9× higher** than the contemporary DBCG IMN2 (mean heart dose 1.2 Gy right, 2.3 Gy left sided).

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** overall survival. Secondary: DFS, DMFS, breast-cancer vs non-breast-cancer mortality, late toxicity, second cancers.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **Primary OS null at 20y** (HR 1.00). The 15y breast-cancer-mortality reduction was offset by excess non-breast-cancer deaths; DFS and DMFS neutral. Numbers in the figures.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: RT-related late effects were more frequent with IM-MS RT (lung fibrosis, cardiac fibrosis, cardiac disease; see figure). **No difference** in second cancers or second breast cancers between arms.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **DBCG IMN2**, using contemporary heart-sparing (heart doses **4-9× lower**), reported reduced distant metastasis, lower breast-cancer mortality, and improved OS in node-positive pts at 15y (Nielsen, Lancet Reg Health Eur 2024). Argues the EORTC null is **technique-dependent**, not concept-dependent.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents node-positive and node-negative stage I-III breast cancer treated with older, higher-heart-dose nodal RT. Does not represent contemporary heart-sparing planning or pN0 patients, in whom added IM-MS RT showed no DFS/DMFS benefit.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Toxicity reflects an older technique with heart doses **4-9× above** modern practice, confounding the OS read. Benefit differs by nodal status. **Dose/fractionation not reported** in source.

open_questions: Does modern low-heart-dose technique preserve IM-MS RT benefit without excess cardiac mortality?
Which nodal subgroups derive net survival benefit from internal mammary irradiation?

studies.name: DBCG HYPO
studies.tldr: 40Gy/15fx reduced 10y grade 2-3 breast induration vs 50Gy/25fx (HR 0.76, p=0.005); recurrence and survival equal.
details: 🔍 Phase III non-inferiority RCT (1:1), N=1882, Denmark/Norway/Germany, 2009-2014
🔍 Node-negative invasive breast cancer or DCIS; whole-breast RT only, no nodal fields
📊 40Gy/15fx significantly reduced 10y grade 2-3 breast induration (primary EP), less fibrosis
📊 No significant difference in locoregional recurrence, distant failure, or breast cancer mortality
📐 Median follow-up 12.8y; prespecified 10-year analysis of toxicity, recurrence, survival
🔗 Confirms START-B (40Gy/15fx) long-term; moderate hypofractionation already the global standard
⚠️ Boost use/fractionation not reported in source
⚠️ Predates ultra-hypofractionation (FAST-Forward 26Gy/5fx); no 5-fraction comparison

verdict.rationale: Phase 3 non-inferiority RCT, 12.8y median f/u, prespecified 10y analysis; reinforces already-standard moderate hypofractionation (START-B). Confirms rather than shifts practice.
verdict.audience: Node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS, adjuvant whole-breast RT

studies.significance: The primary endpoint is fibrosis: 40Gy/15fx is not just non-inferior but superior (HR 0.76, p=0.005 for 10y grade 2-3 induration), with no cost in recurrence or survival. Cements moderate hypofractionation as default for node-negative breast or DCIS.
studies.monday_clinic: In node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS getting adjuvant whole-breast RT, this supports 40Gy/15fx over 50Gy/25fx; it does not extend to node-positive disease needing regional nodal irradiation or to 5-fraction schedules.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 randomised **non-inferiority** trial, 1:1, N=1882, across Denmark, Norway, Germany (2009-2014). Prespecified 10-year analysis; median follow-up **12.8 years**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Node-negative** invasive breast cancer or DCIS. 949 randomised to 50Gy/25fx, 933 to 40Gy/15fx.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Whole-breast RT: **50 Gy/25 fx** vs **40 Gy/15 fx**. Node-negative, so no regional nodal irradiation. Boost use not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** 3-year grade ≥2 breast induration (two consecutive visits or final f/u). Prespecified 10y secondary: toxicity, recurrence, survival.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Consistent with **START-B**, which set 40Gy/15fx as the UK/global standard. Adds mature **10-year** fibrosis and cancer-control data.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS receiving adjuvant whole-breast RT. Does not represent node-positive disease or patients needing regional nodal irradiation.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Boost fractionation not reported in source. Predates **5-fraction** ultra-hypofractionation (FAST-Forward); does not address that schedule.

open_questions: Long-term toxicity of 5-fraction vs 40Gy/15fx whole-breast RT
Fractionation for node-positive disease needing regional nodal irradiation

📊 Most iLRR fell within treated volumes → ESTRO-guided contouring adequately covered failure sites
📊 Failure patterns not obviously different between 3-wk and 5-wk arms (per-arm counts not reported in source)
🔗 Supports moderate hypofractionation for regional nodal RT, where START-A/B carried limited nodal data
⚠️ Descriptive analysis: no formal between-arm non-inferiority statistic for failure patterns in source

figures.caption: First oncological events (N=118): isolated distant recurrence n=61, second malignancy n=37, isolated LRR n=19, concomitant LRR n=1. iLRR sites: 20/30 in-volume (67%), 19/30 nodal.

verdict.rationale: Pre-planned secondary patterns-of-failure analysis of a phase III RCT; descriptive, no formal between-arm comparison. Aligns with hypofractionation adequacy for regional nodal RT.
verdict.audience: Breast cancer, adjuvant RT + tumour-bed boost including regional nodes

studies.significance: The RT read is in-field failure: 20/30 iLRR sites (67%) fell inside the CTV, 19/30 nodal (mainly axillary L1-L2), and LRR were only 20/118 events. So recurrences track systemic risk, not a geographic miss, and 40Gy/15fx with ESTRO-guided nodal contouring covers the failure sites. Moves the fractionation choice, not target design.
studies.monday_clinic: In breast cancer already committed to regional nodal RT + tumour-bed boost, this backs the shorter 40Gy/15fx schedule over 50Gy/25fx; it does not inform whether to deliver nodal RT at all.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Pre-planned secondary** patterns-of-failure and dosimetric analysis of the **HypoG-01 phase III RCT** (ITT). **N=1,260**, median follow-up **4.8 yrs**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Breast cancer receiving **adjuvant RT + tumour-bed boost**; target volumes including nodal levels contoured per **ESTRO guidelines**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Randomized **40 Gy/15 fx** (3-wk) vs **50 Gy/25 fx** (5-wk), both with a **tumour-bed boost**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: First oncological event: **LRR, distant recurrence, or second malignancy**. LRR classified vs CTV as **in-volume / marginal / out-of-volume**; planned dose at recurrence site estimated on the initial RT-plan CT.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **118 first events**: IDR n=61, second malignancy n=37, isolated LRR n=19, concomitant LRR n=1, so **LRR only 20/118**. Of iLRR sites, **20/30 (67%) in-volume**, 19/30 nodal (axillary L1-L2). Patterns **not obviously different** between arms.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Extends hypofractionation confidence to **regional nodal RT**, where **START-A/B** carried limited nodal data; in-field coverage **adequate at 40 Gy/15 fx**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents breast cancer receiving **adjuvant regional RT + tumour-bed boost** at 40 or 50 Gy with ESTRO-guided contouring. Does not represent **five-fraction schedules** or the question of **nodal-RT omission**.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Descriptive** patterns-of-failure analysis, **no formal non-inferiority statistic** between arms in source. Only 118 events; **per-arm event counts not shown**.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Local control is **not the limiting factor**: most first events were distant (n=61) or second malignancy (n=37), while **LRR was 20/118 and mostly in-volume**. Failures track **systemic/biologic risk and adequate contouring**, not fractionation.

studies.name: Tumour Bed Boost (Dutch Breast Cancer RT Registry)
studies.tldr: 10yr IBTR 1.2% with or without boost in 0-2 risk-factor pts, supporting boost omission; only ≥3 factors keep it debatable.
details: 🔍 Retrospective population registry (Dutch Breast Cancer RT Research Group), BCS + whole-breast RT ± tumour-bed boost, treated 2012-2016; primary outcome cumulative IBTR at 5/10yr

details.text: 🔍 Cohort size by boost use and risk-factor count

details: 📐 Assisi omission thresholds applied: 10yr IBTR <3% if boosted, <6% if unboosted
🔗 EORTC 22881/10882 (Bartelink, Lancet Oncol 2015): boost cuts IBTR ~50%, but absolute benefit shrinks as baseline rates fall in the modern systemic era
📊 Every subgroup stayed under the Assisi thresholds except ≥3 risk factors treated with a boost at 10yr
⚠️ Confounding by indication: boost was preferentially given to higher-risk pts, so the raw ≥3-factor comparison (boost worse than no-boost) can't be read as boost being ineffective
⚠️ IBTR ascertained by a pathology-report code/text algorithm, not central pathology review
⚠️ 2012-2016 accrual: 10yr estimates rest on the earliest-treated pts; RT dose/fractionation and boost technique not reported in source

verdict.rationale: Large real-world registry, retrospective so boost allocation is confounded by indication; low absolute IBTR supports emerging boost-omission, consistent with 2024 Assisi thresholds.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, breast-conserving surgery + whole-breast RT

studies.significance: The low-risk read: 10yr IBTR is 1.2% with or without a boost in 0-2 risk-factor pts, so the boost adds no local control and comes off cleanly. The ≥3-factor signal where boost looks worse (3.3% vs 2.7%) is confounding by indication, not boost failure, so that group stays a judgment call.
studies.monday_clinic: In early breast cancer with 0-2 risk factors (age ≤40, grade 3, TNBC, undertreated systemic disease, no pCR) after BCS plus whole-breast RT, this supports omitting the tumour-bed boost; it does not resolve the ≥3-factor group, where boost use stays open.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Retrospective **population-based registry** (Dutch Breast Cancer RT Research Group), pts treated **2012-2016** with breast-conserving surgery plus whole-breast RT, boost given at clinician discretion. Primary outcome: cumulative incidence of IBTR at 5 and 10 years.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Dutch breast cancer pts after breast-conserving treatment, with or without tumour-bed boost. Stratified by count of risk factors: **age ≤40, grade 3, triple-negative, guideline-indicated systemic therapy not adequately given, or no pCR after neoadjuvant chemo in TNBC/HER2+**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Whole-breast RT ± sequential tumour-bed boost. **Boost allocation was non-randomised**, at clinician discretion. Dose, fractionation, and boost technique not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** histologically confirmed IBTR, ascertained via a pathology-report code/text algorithm. Cumulative incidence reported by risk-factor burden at 5 and 10 years.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: In 0-2 risk-factor pts the boost added **no local-control benefit**; only ≥3-factor pts treated with a boost crossed the Assisi 10yr omission threshold, and that arm was higher-risk by selection.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **EORTC 22881/10882** established boost cuts IBTR ~50%, strongest in younger pts. Here absolute 10yr IBTR is low across subgroups, shrinking that relative benefit to a negligible absolute in low-risk pts. Aligns with the 2024 Assisi think-tank omission thresholds.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents early breast cancer after breast-conserving surgery plus whole-breast RT, low-to-moderate risk. Does not represent the ≥3-risk-factor group, where boost benefit stays unresolved, nor any randomised boost-omission comparison.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Retrospective and **non-randomised**: boost was preferentially given to higher-risk pts, confounding the ≥3-factor comparison. IBTR captured algorithmically, not by central review. 10yr estimates rest on the earliest-treated pts.

sites.intro: Real-world SABR breadth (OligoCare, 2447 pts) alongside EXTEND's basket MDT randomisation, though EXTEND's effect size wasn't in source.

studies.name: OligoCare
studies.tldr: Local in-field progression 5.0% at 1yr, 11.4% at 3yr across 2447 real-world SABR oligomet pts; colorectal worst, minimum PTV dose predicts control.
details: 🔍 EORTC OLIGOCARE prospective registry: 57 institutions, 2447 eligible pts, 3533 SABR-treated lesions (Jul 2019-Jul 2025)
🔍 Median age 69 (range 28-94), 69% male; median f/u 31 mo (min 6)
📊 Minimum PTV dose correlated with outcome, the most critical technical factor for local control
📊 De novo OMD had better local control than repeat OMD, attributed to higher delivered dose
⚠️ Colorectal the clear local-control outlier despite the highest median dose per fraction → relative radioresistance
🔗 Fits the SABR-COMET-era ablative metastasis-directed paradigm; adds real-world breadth but no randomised comparator
⚠️ Single-arm registry, interim analysis, no control; heterogeneous primaries, dose, and fractionation across 57 sites
⚠️ 'Minimum PTV dose' is an associative predictor, not a validated prescription threshold; hypothesis-generating

verdict.rationale: Prospective real-world registry, single-arm interim analysis, no comparator; heterogeneous primaries and dose. Descriptive local-control outcomes, not a hypothesis test. Consistent with established ablative-SABR paradigm.
verdict.audience: SABR for oligometastatic disease; mixed primaries, median age 69

studies.significance: The actionable RT lever is coverage dose, not fractionation: minimum PTV dose (not total dose or dose/fraction) tracked local control across 2447 lesions. Colorectal mets failed most despite the highest dose/fraction, flagging radioresistance and a case for dose escalation or systemic combination.
studies.monday_clinic: In colorectal oligomets referred for SABR, this registry flags the highest in-field failure (19.6% at 3yr) despite the highest dose, questioning a standard SABR schedule alone; it does not extend to prostate or breast oligomets, which controlled well.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Prospective real-world **EORTC OLIGOCARE** registry, **57 institutions**, enrolled Jul 2019-Jul 2025. **N=2447** eligible pts, **3533 SABR-treated lesions**. Single-arm, no comparator; interim analysis. Median follow-up **31 mo** (min 6).

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Oligometastatic disease treated with SABR across mixed primaries: prostate 1021, NSCLC 530, colorectal 518, breast 378. Median age **69** (28-94), 69% male. Includes both de novo and repeat OMD.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: SABR to oligomet lesions; **dose/fractionation not standardized** across sites. **Minimum PTV dose** was the strongest technical predictor of local control. The CRC cohort received the highest median dose/fraction yet had the worst control.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Overall local in-field progression **5.0% at 1yr** (99% CI 4.2-6.0), **11.4% at 3yr** (99% CI 10.0-12.9); **88.6% local control at 3yr**, 237 events across 2447 pts.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Aligns with the SABR-COMET-era paradigm of ablative metastasis-directed RT and prospective oligomet series showing durable local control. Adds real-world breadth (2447 pts) but carries **no randomised comparator**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pts receiving SABR for oligometastatic disease across prostate, NSCLC, colorectal, and breast primaries. Does not represent polymetastatic disease or non-SABR local therapies.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Single-arm registry, no control; **interim analysis**, not final. Heterogeneous primaries, dose, and fractionation across 57 sites. The de novo vs repeat OMD gap is confounded by delivered dose; the minimum-PTV-dose signal is associative.

The study involves head and neck squamous cell carcinomas (HNSCC) of the oral cavity, oropharynx, larynx or hypopharynx with positive nodes on only one side of the neck and no distant metastasis treated by primary (chemo)radiotherapy.

Post-hoc analysis of metastasis-free survival
(MFS) and Eugonadal MFS in the RADIOSA phase
Il randomized trial
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
ESTRO
2026
0000g
Background
Metastasis-free survival (MFS) has been validated as a surrogate endpoint for overall survival in non-metastatic prostate
cancer, and may represent an adaptable and clinically meaningful endpoint in oligorecurrent disease.
The phase II RADIOSA trial compared stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) alone (Arm A) versus SBRT plus 6-month
androgen deprivation therapy (ADT, Arm B). This post-hoe analysis evaluated MFS and eugonadal MFS after
testosterone recovery, with a visual mapping of disease trajectories through a Sankey diagram.
Methods
A total of 102 patients were randomized 1:l to Arm A (SBRT) or Arm B (SBRT + ADT).
Median follow-up (Reverse KM) was 49.23 months (95% C1 42.47-54.8).
MFS was defined as the time between randomisation and the appearance of a metastatic recurrence (any MI) on imaging.
Eugonadal MFS was calculated from testosterone recovery to new metastasis or last follow-up.
Kaplan-Meier (KM) survival curves were compared with log-rank tests; hazard ratios were estimated using Cox models.

All patients enrolled in arm B, except two, reached
testosterone recovery within the follow-up period. After
testosterone recovery, eugonadal MFS
remained
significantly longer in Arm B (Figure 1b, p < 0.05).
confirming that the advantage of the combination persisted
beyond

castration.

Study aim
Given that a boost reduces the risk of IBTR by approximately 50%' and guided by the Assisi
thresholds?, the objective of our study is:
To determine 5-and 10-year IBTR rates in patients treated with or without a radiotherapy boost
To identify subgroups of patients in whom a boost may be omitted, i.e.
• Treated with a boost who have 10-year IBTR rates of <3%
• Treated without a boost who have 10-year IBTR rates of <6%

Conclusions
• Low IBTR rates across all patient subgroups
• Based on the presence of combined risk factors, 5-and 10-year IBTR rates exceeded
the Assisi thresholds only in:
• Patients with ≥ 3 risk factors treated with a boost at 10 years
Take home messages
• The low IBTR rates across all subgroups suggest that
• In patients with 0-2 risk factors a tumour bed boost can be omitted, in the modern
systemic therapy era
• In patients with ≥ 3 risk factors the clinical relevance of a tumour bed boost remains
open to discussion
• Further research is needed to identify specific subgroups of patients who benefit from a
boost

5 fraction prostate and pelvic nodal (PPN) SBRT is feasible and safe in a
multicentre setting
Compared with prostate-only SBRT, PPN-SBRT increases GI side effects
in the short-term, but symptoms resolved quickly with no difference at 12
weeks

(EPIC-26 bowel domain) at 4 weeks
No differences in acute GU side effects (clinician or patient-reported)
were seen
Ongoing follow-up will provide data on late effects and cancer control

Background
Methods

Background & Methods
Background
Moderately hypofractionated adjuvant whole-breast radiotherapy for early
node-negative breast cancer is standard worldwide, but long-term
randomised evidence remains limited. Here, we present the Danish
Breast Cancer Group HYPO trial prespecified 10-year outcomes.
Methods
• Phase III randomised non-inferiority trial (1:1) across Denmark,
Norway and Germany (2009-2014)
• 1,882 women with node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
• Whole-breast RT: 50 Gy/25 fractions vs 40 Gy/15 fractions
• Primary endpoint: 3-year grade 22 breast induration at two consequtive
visits, or at final follow-up
• Morbidity evaluated year 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 10
• These results are the prespecified 10-year analyses of toxicity.

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
40 Gy / 15 fractions reduced long-term breast
induration without compromising recurrence or
survival outcomes.
At 12.8 years follow-up, results showed:
• Lower fibrosis risk with maintained cancer outcomes
• Supports continued use of moderately hypofractionated
whole-breast radiotherapy

'ESTRO

0000g
• At 10 years, IBTR event incidence is low in this higher-than-average risk breast cancer
group, whether boost is delivered sequentially or simultaneously
• Incidence in both 40+16Gy and 48Gy groups at 10 years remains lower than the 5%
estimate for control group at 5 years in original sample size calculations
• SIB is a safe treatment with reduced patient visits
• Further escalation of boost dose does not appear advantageous
• These results reaffirm use of 48Gy SIB in this population as standard of care

Discussion
• OLIGOMA is the first RCT to demonstrate a PFS-benefit from MDT in
patients with oligometastatic breast cancer.
• Majority had ER/PR pos. HER2 neg. disease in the first line-setting
• >80% of patients had 1-3 metastases, 2/3 of lesions were bone metastases.
• Short-term quality of life was not impaired by MDT.
• Recruitment was stopped early (< 20% of initial, < 40% of amended #)
• Numerous trials are still ongoing (incl. TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA,
CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI).
• Comprehensive analyses are needed to determine which patients benefit
most.

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
"ESTRO
2026
At 15 years median of follow up, the phase III APBI-IMRT Florence trial (NCT02104895)
demonstrates durable oncological outcomes with PB in selected patients with early breast cancer
Although a numerical increase in IBTR was observed, this was largely attributable to new
primary tumours rather than true local recurrences.
No differences were observed in loco-regional control, distant metastasis, breast cancer-
specific survival or overall survival.
These findings provide mature evidence supporting PBl as a safe de-escalation strategy and
reinforce its role as a standard treatment option in appropriately selected patients
